questions.q: Which horn-extension property characterizes a left fibration $p: \mathcal{E} \to \mathcal{B}$?
choices: Every inner horn $\Lambda^n_k$ ($0 < k < n$) extends.
Every horn (inner or outer) extends — i.e. $p$ is a Kan fibration.
questions.explain: By definition, $p$ is a left fibration iff every diagram $\Lambda^n_0 \to \mathcal{E}$ together with a chosen extension $\Delta^n \to \mathcal{B}$ admits a filler $\Delta^n \to \mathcal{E}$ for every $n \geq 1$. Inner horns characterize inner fibrations (and quasi-categories when $\mathcal{B} = *$); right horns characterize right fibrations.
questions.hint: $\Lambda^n_0$ is the horn missing the face opposite the initial vertex.

questions.q: Select every map that is a left fibration.
choices: The fold $\Delta^1 \sqcup \Delta^1 \to \Delta^1$ identifying the two copies vertex-to-vertex.
The unique map from a Kan complex $X$ to $\Delta^0$.
questions.explain: Identity-style maps and Kan fibrations satisfy every horn condition, so they are left fibrations a fortiori. A Kan complex over a point is the same data as a Kan fibration to $\Delta^0$. The boundary inclusion is not even surjective in dimension $2$ — it does not extend the constant horn at the missing $2$-simplex. The fold map is not a fibration of any kind.
questions.hint: Every Kan fibration is a left fibration; a left fibration over a point is precisely a Kan complex.

questions.q: Under straightening, a left fibration over $\mathcal{B}$ corresponds to:
choices: A functor $\mathcal{B} \to \mathcal{S}$ (a covariant family of spaces).
questions.explain: Left fibrations over $\mathcal{B}$ straighten to functors $\mathcal{B} \to \mathcal{S}$. Right fibrations over $\mathcal{B}$ straighten to $\mathcal{B}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathcal{S}$, recovering the classical Yoneda picture of presheaves of spaces. Cocartesian / cartesian fibrations are the $\mathrm{Cat}_\infty$-valued versions.
questions.hint: The fibers of a left fibration are spaces, not categories; covariance comes from $\Lambda^n_0$.

hard.q: Below is a sketch that 'every left fibration is a right fibration.' Find the false step.
steps: Suppose $p: \mathcal{E} \to \mathcal{B}$ is a left fibration: every $\Lambda^n_0$-horn lifts.
Reverse every $1$-simplex in $\mathcal{B}$ and in $\mathcal{E}$ — i.e. apply the $\mathrm{op}$ functor coordinatewise.
Since $\Lambda^n_0$ becomes $\Lambda^n_n$ under $\mathrm{op}$, $p^{\mathrm{op}}$ is a right fibration; this proves the claim for $p^{\mathrm{op}}$, hence for $p$.
Therefore $p$ itself is a right fibration.
hard.explain: Steps 1–3 are correct: $p$ a left fibration is equivalent to $p^{\mathrm{op}}$ a right fibration. Step 4 silently identifies $p$ with $p^{\mathrm{op}}$, which is wrong: the two are opposite maps, not equal. The true statement is the duality between left and right fibrations, not their coincidence.

hard.q: Joyal's recognition theorem says: a left fibration $p: \mathcal{E} \to \mathcal{B}$ is a trivial Kan fibration iff…
choices: Every fiber $\mathcal{E}_b$ is a contractible Kan complex.
$\mathcal{B}$ is the nerve of an ordinary category.
hard.explain: A left fibration with contractible fibers is a trivial Kan fibration: contractibility kills all higher homotopy, so the fibration is in fact an equivalence. Existence of a section is necessary but far from sufficient; the base being a $1$-categorical nerve has no bearing on triviality.

questions.q: An edge $e: x \to y$ in $\mathcal{E}$ over $\bar{e}: \bar{x} \to \bar{y}$ is $p$-cocartesian iff:
choices: For every $z \in \mathcal{E}$, post-composition with $e$ induces an equivalence $\mathrm{Map}_\mathcal{E}(y, z) \to \mathrm{Map}_\mathcal{E}(x, z) \times_{\mathrm{Map}_\mathcal{B}(\bar{x}, p(z))} \mathrm{Map}_\mathcal{B}(\bar{y}, p(z))$.
questions.explain: The cocartesian condition is exactly the universal lifting property: $e$ is cocartesian over $\bar{e}$ iff for every $z$, the induced square on mapping spaces is a pullback in spaces. Equivalently, every $\Lambda^{n+1}_0$-horn whose initial edge is $e$ lifts uniquely (up to contractible choice). Cocartesian edges need not be invertible.
questions.hint: The defining property is universal: extending $e$ to higher simplices upstairs is determined by the same data downstairs.

questions.q: A claimed proof that 'every edge over an identity is cocartesian' goes:
steps: Let $\bar{e} = \mathrm{id}_{\bar{x}}$ and let $e: x \to y$ in $\mathcal{E}$ with $p(e) = \bar{e}$.
Then $e$ lies in the fiber $\mathcal{E}_{\bar{x}}$, which is an $\infty$-subcategory of $\mathcal{E}$.
An edge inside the fiber is automatically a 'fiberwise' edge, i.e. lifts every horn whose base is in $\mathcal{E}_{\bar{x}}$.
Therefore $e$ is $p$-cocartesian for every such fibration $p$.
questions.explain: Step 4 confuses 'fiberwise lifting' with the cocartesian condition. The cocartesian property concerns horns whose base extends off the fiber — i.e. into edges of $\mathcal{B}$ starting from $\bar{x}$. A fiber-internal edge is cocartesian iff it is an equivalence in the fiber $\mathcal{E}_{\bar{x}}$ (HTT 2.4.1.5). A non-equivalence in the fiber is a perfectly valid edge over $\mathrm{id}_{\bar{x}}$ and is not cocartesian.

questions.q: If $e_1, e_2: x \to y$ are both $p$-cocartesian over the same $\bar{e}: \bar{x} \to \bar{y}$, what is the relationship between them?
choices: They are equivalent in the fiber $\mathcal{E}_{\bar{y}}$ via a canonical equivalence in $\mathrm{Map}_{\mathcal{E}_{\bar{y}}}(y, y)$.
They are equivalent in $\mathcal{E}$, but the equivalence need not lie in any fiber.
They become equal after passing to the homotopy category $h(\mathcal{E})$.
questions.hint: Cocartesian lifts are unique up to a contractible space of choices, so any two cocartesian lifts of $\bar{e}$ starting at $x$ are linked by an equivalence between their endpoints — and that…
questions.explain: Cocartesian lifts are unique up to a contractible space of choices, so any two cocartesian lifts of $\bar{e}$ starting at $x$ are linked by an equivalence between their endpoints — and that equivalence sits in the fiber $\mathcal{E}_{\bar{y}}$ (since both endpoints live there). Equality on the nose is far too strong; the homotopy-category quotient is too coarse.

questions.q: $p: \mathcal{E} \to \mathcal{B}$ is a cocartesian fibration iff $p$ is an inner fibration and:
choices: For every edge $\bar{e}: \bar{x} \to \bar{y}$ of $\mathcal{B}$ and every $x \in \mathcal{E}_{\bar{x}}$, there exists a $p$-cocartesian edge $e: x \to y$ with $p(e) = \bar{e}$.
Every fiber $\mathcal{E}_{\bar{b}}$ is a Kan complex.
questions.explain: The standard definition (HTT 2.4.2.1, Kerodon 5.1.4): inner fibration plus the cocartesian-lift existence axiom. The fibers being Kan complexes characterizes left fibrations among cocartesian ones; every-edge-cocartesian forces $p$ to factor through an equivalence onto its image.
questions.hint: Two clauses: 'inner fibration' (so fibers are $\infty$-categories) and 'cocartesian lifts always exist'.

questions.q: Which of the following are cocartesian fibrations?
choices: The forgetful $\mathrm{Mod}_R \to \mathrm{CAlg}$ from modules to commutative ring spectra.
The equalizer inclusion $\mathrm{eq}(f, g) \hookrightarrow \mathcal{C}$ for parallel functors $f, g: \mathcal{C} \to \mathcal{D}$.
questions.explain: Source-of-an-arrow, product projection, and the modules-over-CAlg fibration are the three canonical examples (Kerodon 5.1.5). The diagonal is generally not even an inner fibration. An equalizer inclusion is a monomorphism, not a fibration; it has no nontrivial cocartesian lifts.
questions.hint: Cocartesian fibrations classify covariant families of $\infty$-categories — projections and arrow-source maps fit; inclusions and diagonals don't.

questions.q: For the projection $p: \mathcal{C} \times \mathcal{D} \to \mathcal{C}$, what is the transport functor along $\bar{e}: c \to c'$?
choices: Postcomposition with $\bar{e}$, viewed as a functor $\mathcal{D} \to \mathcal{D}$.
The constant functor $\mathcal{D} \to \mathcal{D}$ at the initial object.
Pullback along $\bar{e}$ — only defined when $\mathcal{D}$ has fiber products.
questions.explain: The fiber over each $c$ is $\mathcal{D}$ itself, and the cocartesian lift of $\bar{e}: c \to c'$ at $(c, d)$ is the edge $(\bar{e}, \mathrm{id}_d): (c, d) \to (c', d)$. So $\bar{e}_!: \mathcal{D} \to \mathcal{D}$ is the identity, and the straightening is the constant functor $\mathcal{C} \to \mathrm{Cat}_\infty$ with value $\mathcal{D}$.
questions.hint: A constant family of fibers ought to have constant transport.

hard.q: If $p: \mathcal{E} \to \mathcal{B}$ is both a cocartesian fibration and a cartesian fibration, then $p$ encodes:
choices: An adjunction in the sense of HTT 5.2 — both fibrations together package the unit and counit.
Necessarily a Kan fibration (so all fibers are Kan complexes).
Nothing nontrivial — every cocartesian fibration is automatically cartesian.
hard.explain: A biCartesian fibration $p: \mathcal{M} \to \Delta^1$ is exactly Lurie's encoding of an $\infty$-adjunction (HTT 5.2.2.1): the cocartesian transport $0 \to 1$ supplies the left adjoint, and the cartesian transport $1 \to 0$ supplies the right adjoint, with the joint structure encoding the unit + counit. Over a more general base it encodes a fiberwise adjunction.

hard.q: Match each cocartesian fibration $p$ to the corresponding straightened functor $\mathcal{B} \to \mathrm{Cat}_\infty$.
hard.explain: (left index → right slot.) Constant family ↔ product projection (idx 3 of left = $\mathcal{C} \times \mathcal{D}$). Slice functor ↔ arrow-source (left idx 1 → right idx 0, since right[0] is the modules fibration — wait, double-check: right[0] is Mod$\to$CAlg → matches Module functor (left idx 2). The intended answer pairs are: 'constant $\mathcal{D}$' ↔ '$\mathcal{C} \times \mathcal{D} \to \mathcal{C}$', 'slice $c \mapsto \mathcal{C}_{c/}$' ↔ '$s: \mathrm{Fun}(\Delta^1,\mathcal{C}) \to \mathcal{C}$', 'modules $R \mapsto \mathrm{Mod}_R$' ↔ '$\mathrm{Mod} \to \mathrm{CAlg}$', 'BG-action on $X$' ↔ '$X /\!\!/ G \to BG$'. Each is a canonical example of straightening.

questions.q: For the source map $s: \mathrm{Fun}(\Delta^1, \mathcal{C}) \to \mathcal{C}$, what is the fiber over an object $x \in \mathcal{C}$?
questions.explain: An object of $\mathrm{Fun}(\Delta^1, \mathcal{C})$ is an arrow $f: a \to b$; the source map sends $f$ to $a$. The fiber over $x$ is therefore the $\infty$-category of arrows with source $x$, i.e. the slice $\mathcal{C}_{x/}$. (The target map gives $\mathcal{C}_{/x}$.) The transport functor along $\bar{e}: x \to y$ is the postcomposition $\bar{e}_*: \mathcal{C}_{x/} \to \mathcal{C}_{y/}$ … wait, careful: cocartesian lift of $\bar{e}$ at an arrow $f: x \to z$ is the $2$-simplex with $f$ on one face and $\bar{e}$ on another, giving an arrow $y \to z$ — i.e. precomposition by $\bar{e}$, sending $f$ to $f \circ \bar{e}^{-1}$ … no: source map is cocartesian, so $\bar{e}_!$ on $\mathcal{C}_{x/}$ goes to $\mathcal{C}_{y/}$ via filling. The end product is precomposition with $\bar{e}$ in the contravariant direction.
questions.hint: An arrow $f: a \to b$ has source $a$; the source-fiber over $x$ is what one builds out of arrows starting at $x$.

questions.q: Given $\bar{e}: \bar{x} \to \bar{y}$ and $\bar{f}: \bar{y} \to \bar{z}$ in $\mathcal{B}$, the transport functor $(\bar{f} \circ \bar{e})_!$ is canonically equivalent to:
choices: Neither — the transport is only well-defined on individual edges, not on compositions.
questions.explain: Transport is a (lax / pseudo) functorial assignment: composition of edges in $\mathcal{B}$ becomes composition of transport functors in $\mathrm{Cat}_\infty$, $\bar{f}_! \bar{e}_!$, with a coherent equivalence — this is one face of the straightening equivalence. The order $\bar{f}_! \circ \bar{e}_!$ matches the order of edges $\bar{x} \to \bar{y} \to \bar{z}$.
questions.hint: Same composition order as the underlying edges in $\mathcal{B}$.

questions.q: If $\bar{e}: \bar{x} \to \bar{y}$ is an equivalence in $\mathcal{B}$, then $\bar{e}_!: \mathcal{E}_{\bar{x}} \to \mathcal{E}_{\bar{y}}$ is:
choices: The constant functor at the initial object.
Always the identity (after identifying the fibers).
Defined only up to a non-trivial 2-cell, not as a functor.
questions.explain: Functoriality of transport sends equivalences in $\mathcal{B}$ to equivalences in $\mathrm{Cat}_\infty$, so $\bar{e}_!$ is itself an equivalence with inverse $\bar{e}^{-1}_!$. Identifying it with the literal identity requires choosing a path between $\bar{x}$ and $\bar{y}$ — the equivalence depends on the chosen lift.

hard.q: For the modules fibration $\mathrm{Mod}_R \to \mathrm{CAlg}$, what is the transport functor $\bar{f}_!$ along a ring map $\bar{f}: R \to S$?
hard.explain: Cocartesian transport along $R \to S$ in the modules fibration is base change $S \otimes_R^{\mathbb{L}} -$. Restriction of scalars goes the other way (it's the cartesian transport, since the fibration is also cartesian — modules-over-CAlg is a biCartesian fibration whose two transports are an adjoint pair $S \otimes_R - \dashv \mathrm{res}$).
hard.hint: Base change is left adjoint to restriction; cocartesian = left adjoint side.

hard.q: Find the false step in this 'computation' of the fiber of the target map $t: \mathrm{Fun}(\Delta^1, \mathcal{C}) \to \mathcal{C}$ over $y$:
steps: An object of $\mathrm{Fun}(\Delta^1, \mathcal{C})$ is an edge $f: a \to b$.
So $t^{-1}(y)$ consists of edges $f$ with $b = y$, i.e. arrows ending at $y$.
These form the slice $\mathcal{C}_{y/}$ — arrows out of $y$.
hard.explain: Step 4 mislabels the slice. Arrows ending at $y$ — i.e. $a \to y$ — form $\mathcal{C}_{/y}$, the over-category of $y$. The slice $\mathcal{C}_{y/}$ contains arrows starting at $y$. So the target-fiber is $\mathcal{C}_{/y}$ (and the source-fiber is $\mathcal{C}_{y/}$); confusing the two is the standard slip.

questions.q: Lurie's straightening equivalence sends a cocartesian fibration $p: \mathcal{E} \to \mathcal{B}$ to:
choices: A pair of adjoint functors between $\mathcal{E}$ and $\mathcal{B}$.
questions.explain: The cocartesian-fibration straightening is covariant: $\mathrm{St}(p): \mathcal{B} \to \mathrm{Cat}_\infty$, $\bar{b} \mapsto \mathcal{E}_{\bar{b}}$, $\bar{e} \mapsto \bar{e}_!$. Cartesian fibrations straighten contravariantly — to $\mathcal{B}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{Cat}_\infty$.

questions.q: Restricted to ordinary categories (not $\infty$-categories), the Grothendieck construction recovers:
choices: The classical equivalence between Grothendieck fibrations over $\mathcal{C}$ and pseudofunctors $\mathcal{C} \to \mathrm{Cat}$.
The Quillen equivalence between simplicial sets and topological spaces.
questions.explain: Restricting both sides to nerves of ordinary categories recovers the classical statement (Grothendieck SGA 1, Exp. VI): Grothendieck (op-)fibrations over $\mathcal{C}$ = pseudofunctors $\mathcal{C} \to \mathrm{Cat}$. The $\infty$-version is its homotopy-coherent generalization with strict pseudofunctoriality replaced by coherence-up-to-higher-simplices.

questions.q: Unstraightening applied to the constant functor $\mathcal{B} \to \mathrm{Cat}_\infty$ at $\mathcal{D}$ produces:
questions.explain: A constant family of fibers all equal to $\mathcal{D}$ unstraightens to the trivial fibration $\mathcal{B} \times \mathcal{D} \to \mathcal{B}$, with all transport functors equal to $\mathrm{id}_\mathcal{D}$.

hard.q: A morphism $p \to q$ of cocartesian fibrations over $\mathcal{B}$ — i.e. a commuting triangle $\mathcal{E} \to \mathcal{F}$ over $\mathcal{B}$ that preserves cocartesian edges — corresponds under straightening to:
choices: Strictly nothing — only the fibrations themselves correspond, not their morphisms.
A natural transformation that is an equivalence on every component.
hard.explain: Straightening is a functor of $\infty$-categories, sending the $\infty$-category $\mathrm{coCart}(\mathcal{B})$ of cocartesian fibrations and cocartesian-edge-preserving maps to $\mathrm{Fun}(\mathcal{B}, \mathrm{Cat}_\infty)$. The cocartesian-preservation hypothesis is essential — without it, one obtains an arbitrary morphism over $\mathcal{B}$, which corresponds to a lax natural transformation rather than a genuine one.
hard.hint: It's an equivalence of $\infty$-categories, so morphisms correspond to morphisms — between functor $\infty$-categories.

hard.q: How does straightening interact with the Cartesian product of fibrations? Concretely: if $p: \mathcal{E} \to \mathcal{B}$ and $q: \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{B}$ are cocartesian, the fiberwise product $p \times_\mathcal{B} q: \mathcal{E} \times_\mathcal{B} \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{B}$ corresponds to:
choices: Generally undefined — fiberwise products of cocartesian fibrations need not be cocartesian.
hard.explain: Straightening preserves limits (it's an equivalence of presentable $\infty$-categories), so the fiber product over $\mathcal{B}$ corresponds to the pointwise product of the straightenings as objects of $\mathrm{Fun}(\mathcal{B}, \mathrm{Cat}_\infty)$. The fiberwise product is automatically cocartesian whenever both factors are.

questions.q: Restricting straightening to left fibrations gives an equivalence of $\infty$-categories:
questions.explain: Left fibrations have spaces as fibers (their inner-fibration property + every-edge-cocartesian forces the fibers to be Kan complexes), so straightening lands in $\mathrm{Fun}(\mathcal{B}, \mathcal{S})$ — covariant functors to spaces. Right fibrations give $\mathcal{B}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathcal{S}$, i.e. presheaves.
questions.hint: Left fibration ↔ covariant; presheaf is contravariant — that's right fibrations.

questions.q: Yoneda's $\infty$-categorical embedding $\mathcal{B} \to \mathcal{P}(\mathcal{B}) = \mathrm{Fun}(\mathcal{B}^{\mathrm{op}}, \mathcal{S})$ factors through:
choices: The universal right fibration over $\mathcal{B}$ — i.e. the target map $\mathrm{tw}(\mathcal{B}) \to \mathcal{B}$ of the twisted-arrow $\infty$-category projected onto its source-side.
questions.explain: Yoneda factors through the universal right fibration: an object $b \in \mathcal{B}$ is sent to the slice $\mathcal{B}_{/b}$, which is the fiber of the right fibration $\mathrm{ev}_1: \mathrm{Fun}(\Delta^1, \mathcal{B}) \to \mathcal{B}$ over $b$. Straightening this right fibration recovers the Yoneda functor $\mathcal{B} \to \mathcal{P}(\mathcal{B})$.
questions.hint: $\mathcal{B}_{/b}$ = the representable presheaf at $b$, viewed as the fiber of a fibration.

questions.q: A left fibration $p: \mathcal{E} \to \mathcal{B}$ is the same data as a cocartesian fibration whose:
choices: Fibers are empty for all but one object of $\mathcal{B}$.
questions.explain: A cocartesian fibration with $\mathcal{S}$-valued fibers — equivalently, all fibers being Kan complexes — is exactly a left fibration. The straightening lands in $\mathrm{Fun}(\mathcal{B}, \mathcal{S})$ rather than $\mathrm{Fun}(\mathcal{B}, \mathrm{Cat}_\infty)$ when this happens (HTT 2.1.1.2).

hard.q: For $\mathcal{B} = \Delta^0$ (the point), which $\infty$-categories admit the structure of any left fibration over $\Delta^0$?
choices: Exactly the nerves of ordinary categories.
hard.explain: A left fibration over a point is the same data as a Kan complex — its fiber, which is forced to be an $\infty$-groupoid by the every-edge-cocartesian condition specialized to $\mathcal{B} = \Delta^0$. This recovers the equivalence $\mathrm{LFib}(\Delta^0) \simeq \mathcal{S}$.

choices: $\mathcal{S}_*/ \to \mathcal{S}$: the slice of pointed spaces over the $\infty$-category of spaces.
$\Delta^1 \to \Delta^0$ — every left fibration pulls back from this.
There is no universal left fibration; the moduli of left fibrations is too large.
hard.explain: The forgetful $\mathcal{S}_{*/} \to \mathcal{S}$ from pointed spaces (or 'spaces with a chosen basepoint') to spaces is the universal left fibration: every left fibration $\mathcal{E} \to \mathcal{B}$ is the pullback of this universal one along its straightening $\mathcal{B} \to \mathcal{S}$. This is a key tool in HTT §3.3.

examples-of-fibrations.title: Examples: arrow categories, modules, classifying spaces

questions.q: Match each canonical fibration to the functor $\mathcal{B} \to \mathrm{Cat}_\infty$ (or $\mathcal{S}$) that it straightens to.
left: $* \mapsto X$ (a $G$-space, viewed via the action $G$-action functor $BG \to \mathcal{S}$)
right: $X /\!\!/ G \to BG$ — the homotopy quotient as a left fibration over $BG$
$\mathrm{id}: \mathcal{C} \to \mathcal{C}$ — every object's fiber is a singleton
$s: \mathrm{Fun}(\Delta^1, \mathcal{C}) \to \mathcal{C}$ — the source map of the arrow category
questions.explain: (left → right indices.) Slice functor straightens to the source map (left 0 → right 3). Modules functor straightens to the modules fibration (left 1 → right 2). $G$-action straightens to homotopy quotient (left 2 → right 0). Constant singleton functor unstraightens to the identity (left 3 → right 1).

questions.q: Among the following projections, which are left fibrations?
choices: $\mathcal{C} \times \mathcal{D} \to \mathcal{C}$ — product projection where $\mathcal{D}$ is an $\infty$-category but not a Kan complex.
questions.explain: $\mathcal{C}_{x/} \to \mathcal{C}$ has Kan-complex fibers $\mathrm{Map}_\mathcal{C}(x, -)$, so it is a left fibration. $X /\!\!/ G \to BG$ has fibers all equivalent to the space $X$, so it is a left fibration. The arrow source-map has $\infty$-category fibers $\mathcal{C}_{x/}$ (not Kan complexes in general), so it's cocartesian but not left. The slice 'forget target' map is a right fibration. A product projection with an $\infty$-category factor is cocartesian, not left.
questions.hint: Left fibration = cocartesian + Kan-complex fibers = $\mathcal{S}$-valued straightening.

questions.q: For the modules fibration $\mathrm{Mod}_R \to \mathrm{CAlg}$, why is base change $S \otimes_R^{\mathbb{L}} -$ rather than ordinary tensor $S \otimes_R -$ the correct transport?
choices: Ordinary tensor doesn't even make sense in spectra.
The fibration is cocartesian in the $\infty$-categorical sense, which builds in the higher coherence that derived (= homotopy-invariant) tensoring provides.
The two functors agree, so it doesn't matter.
Base change is computed using $\mathrm{Tor}$, but the fibration only sees its $0$th cohomology.
questions.hint: The cocartesian-fibration formalism is intrinsically homotopy-coherent: cocartesian lifts are unique only up to (a contractible space of) higher homotopy, so the corresponding base-change functor is…
questions.explain: The cocartesian-fibration formalism is intrinsically homotopy-coherent: cocartesian lifts are unique only up to (a contractible space of) higher homotopy, so the corresponding base-change functor is the derived (= $\mathbb{L}$-tensor) one. Plain $\otimes_R$ would be the wrong functor on $\mathrm{Mod}_R$ at the chain-level, breaking homotopy invariance and failing to give a cocartesian fibration.
